words.shortDefinition: The algorithm used by browsers to determine which CSS rule applies to an element.
words.longDefinition: A weight applied to a given CSS declaration, determined by the number of each selector type in the matching selector. If multiple declarations have the same specificity, the last one found in the CSS is applied.
words.techContext: Crucial for resolving styling conflicts. Inline styles have the highest specificity, followed by IDs, then classes/attributes/pseudo-classes, and finally elements.
words.exampleSentence: The button remained blue even after I added a red rule because the existing ID selector had higher specificity than my new class selector.
words.etymology: from Latin specificus ('forming a particular kind').

words.shortDefinition: A one-dimensional layout model for distributing space and aligning items.
words.simpleDefinition: A CSS layout system that makes it easy to arrange items in rows or columns, automatically distributing space and aligning content.
words.longDefinition: The Flexible Box Layout Module. It provides an efficient way to lay out, align, and distribute space among items in a container, even when their size is unknown or dynamic. It primarily deals with layout in one direction (row or column).
words.techContext: The standard for UI components like navigation bars, centered content, and simple grids.
words.exampleSentence: We used Flexbox to perfectly center the loading spinner both horizontally and vertically within the parent container.
words.connector: Think of Flexbox like organizing items on a shelf — you can space them evenly, align them to one side, or let them wrap to the next line when they run out of room.

words.shortDefinition: A two-dimensional layout system for the web.
words.longDefinition: A powerful layout engine that allows developers to create complex responsive web designs more easily and consistently across browsers. Unlike Flexbox, it handles both columns and rows simultaneously.
words.techContext: Ideal for full-page layouts, dashboard interfaces, and complex masonry-style galleries.
words.exampleSentence: By using CSS Grid, we were able to define our entire page layout—header, sidebar, and content—in just a few lines of code.

words.shortDefinition: The fundamental concept that every element in web design is a rectangular box.
words.simpleDefinition: The concept that every HTML element is a rectangular box made of content, padding, border, and margin — understanding this is key to controlling layout and spacing.
words.longDefinition: Consists of four parts: content, padding, border, and margin. The browser calculates the total size and position of elements based on these values.
words.techContext: Understanding how these layers interact is essential for precise spacing and preventing layout overflows.
words.exampleSentence: The horizontal scrollbar appeared because the padding and border were being added to the fixed width, exceeding the container's bounds.
words.etymology: Descriptive of the nested 'box' structure of elements.
words.connector: Think of the box model like a framed picture — the image is the content, the mat is padding, the frame is the border, and the space between frames on the wall is the margin.

words.shortDefinition: Keywords added to selectors that let you style specific parts of an element.
words.longDefinition: Used to style parts of an element that aren't represented in the DOM, such as the first letter of a paragraph, or to insert content before or after an element (::before, ::after).
words.techContext: Frequently used for decorative icons, clearfixes, or custom bullet points without adding extra HTML nodes.
words.exampleSentence: I used the ::after pseudo-element to add a small arrow icon next to all external links in the article.

words.shortDefinition: Keywords added to selectors that specify a special state of the elements.
words.longDefinition: Used to define the style of an element when a certain condition is met, such as when a user hovers over it, when it's the first child of its parent, or when an input is invalid.
words.techContext: Essential for interactive states (:hover, :focus, :active) and structural selections (:nth-child, :first-of-type).
words.exampleSentence: We used the :nth-child(even) pseudo-class to give alternating colors to the table rows for better readability.

words.shortDefinition: The process of combining multiple style sheets and resolving conflicts.
words.longDefinition: The 'C' in CSS. It defines the priority of CSS rules based on origin (user vs agent vs author), importance (!important), specificity, and source order.
words.techContext: Understanding the cascade is vital for managing large stylesheets and knowing why certain styles are being overridden.
words.exampleSentence: Because of the cascading nature of CSS, the styles defined in our global theme were being overridden by the more specific component styles.
words.etymology: from Italian cascata ('a fall of water').

words.shortDefinition: The mechanism by which certain properties are passed from parent to child elements.
words.longDefinition: Some CSS properties, like color and font-family, are inherited by child elements by default. Others, like borders and margins, are not. You can control this using the 'inherit', 'initial', and 'unset' keywords.
words.techContext: Allows developers to set global styles on the body tag that automatically apply to all text elements in the app.
words.exampleSentence: The paragraph inherited the font family from the body tag, so we didn't have to redefine it for every text element.

words.shortDefinition: A property that controls how the total width and height of an element is calculated.
words.longDefinition: The default 'content-box' excludes padding and borders from width/height. 'border-box' includes them, making it much easier to size elements accurately. Most modern resets set everything to 'border-box'.
words.techContext: Using 'box-sizing: border-box' is a best practice that prevents padding and borders from breaking responsive layouts.
words.exampleSentence: Switching to border-box simplified our calculations since a 50% width element actually took up exactly 50% of the screen, regardless of padding.

words.shortDefinition: A property that sets the stack order of a positioned element.
words.longDefinition: An element with a larger z-index is always in front of an element with a smaller z-index. It only works on positioned elements (relative, absolute, fixed, sticky) and elements in a flex/grid context.
words.techContext: Used to manage the layering of UI elements like modals, tooltips, and sticky headers.
words.exampleSentence: I had to increase the z-index of the navigation bar to ensure it stayed above the scrolling content of the page.
words.etymology: Named after the 'Z-axis' in a 3D coordinate system.

words.shortDefinition: A three-dimensional conceptualization of HTML elements along an imaginary z-axis.
words.longDefinition: A local hierarchy for z-index. Certain properties (like opacity < 1, transform, or position: fixed) create a new stacking context. Elements inside a context are ranked relative to each other, but the entire context is ranked as a single unit in its parent's hierarchy.
words.techContext: The reason why a z-index of 9999 sometimes still fails to bring an element to the front—it might be trapped in a lower stacking context.
words.exampleSentence: The tooltip was appearing behind the sidebar because the sidebar created a new stacking context that the tooltip couldn't break out of.

words.shortDefinition: A technique to apply CSS rules based on device characteristics like screen width.
words.simpleDefinition: CSS rules that apply different styles based on screen size or device features — the foundation of making websites work on phones, tablets, and desktops.
words.longDefinition: Part of CSS3, they allow the presentation of content to be tailored to a range of output devices without changing the content itself. They are the cornerstone of Responsive Web Design.
words.techContext: Used to create 'breakpoints' where the layout shifts from mobile (single column) to desktop (multi-column).
words.exampleSentence: We used media queries to hide the sidebar and show a hamburger menu on screens smaller than 768 pixels.
words.connector: Think of media queries like clothes that adjust to fit — the same website adapts its layout depending on whether you're viewing it on a phone or a widescreen monitor.

words.shortDefinition: Entities defined by CSS authors that contain specific values to be reused throughout a document.
words.longDefinition: Also known as Custom Properties. They are prefixed with -- and accessed using the var() function. Unlike Sass variables, they are dynamic and can be updated at runtime via JavaScript or media queries.
words.techContext: The modern way to implement 'Dark Mode' or consistent design system themes (spacing, colors, typography).
words.exampleSentence: By updating a single CSS variable, we can instantly change the primary brand color across the entire application.

words.shortDefinition: A property that allows you to isolate a subtree of the page from the rest of the page.
words.longDefinition: Using the 'contain' property, you tell the browser that an element's styles, layout, or paint are independent of the rest of the DOM. This allows the browser to skip re-calculating the entire page when only that element changes.
words.techContext: A powerful optimization for complex UIs with many dynamic elements, often used in conjunction with Container Queries.
words.exampleSentence: Applying 'contain: content' to the list items significantly improved scroll performance by isolating their layout from the header.

words.shortDefinition: A value for grid-template-rows/columns that inherits the grid definition of the parent.
words.longDefinition: Allows a nested grid to use the tracks defined on its parent grid. This ensures that elements in different nested components can still align with each other according to a shared layout.
words.techContext: Solves the long-standing problem of aligning cards in a grid where the headers or footers of different cards might have different heights.
words.exampleSentence: By using subgrid, the form labels in all our nested components perfectly align with the main grid columns.

words.shortDefinition: CSS properties that refer to the block and inline axes rather than physical directions.
words.longDefinition: Instead of left, right, top, and bottom, logical properties use 'start' and 'end' relative to the text direction. This makes it much easier to support both Left-to-Right (LTR) and Right-to-Left (RTL) languages.
words.techContext: Using 'margin-inline-start' instead of 'margin-left' ensures your layout works perfectly for both English and Arabic users.
words.exampleSentence: We refactored our design system to use logical properties, so we no longer need separate stylesheets for RTL languages.

words.shortDefinition: A CSS function that clamps a value between an upper and lower bound.
words.longDefinition: The clamp() function takes three parameters: a minimum value, a preferred value, and a maximum allowed value. It's often used for fluid typography that scales with the viewport.
words.techContext: Replaces complex media queries for font sizes, allowing text to grow and shrink smoothly between two extremes.
words.exampleSentence: I used clamp to set the font size so it's never smaller than 1rem, never larger than 3rem, but scales with the screen width in between.

words.shortDefinition: Applying styles based on the size of a parent container rather than the viewport.
words.longDefinition: Allows components to change their layout depending on how much space they have available in their specific parent, making them truly modular and reusable regardless of where they are placed on the page.
words.techContext: The 'holy grail' of responsive design, allowing a card component to look different in a narrow sidebar than it does in a wide main content area.
words.exampleSentence: With container queries, our product card automatically switches to a horizontal layout when placed in a wide container, even on mobile.

words.shortDefinition: The ratio of an element's width to its height.
words.longDefinition: The aspect-ratio CSS property allows you to define a preferred ratio for an element, which the browser will maintain even if one dimension is set to auto or percentage.
words.techContext: Essential for preventing Layout Shift (CLS) by reserving space for images and videos before they load.
words.exampleSentence: By setting aspect-ratio: 16 / 9 on the video container, we prevented the page from jumping when the YouTube player finished loading.

words.shortDefinition: A hint to the browser that an element is expected to change.
words.longDefinition: Allows the browser to perform optimizations (like creating a new compositor layer) before an element actually changes. This can make animations much smoother by doing the heavy lifting in advance.
words.techContext: Should be used sparingly as an 'emergency' optimization, as overusing it can consume significant memory and CPU.
words.exampleSentence: I added will-change: transform to the sliding menu to eliminate the slight stutter at the start of the animation.

words.shortDefinition: CSS units that are relative to the size of the browser window.
words.simpleDefinition: CSS units (vw, vh) that size elements relative to the browser window — 100vw means full window width, 50vh means half window height.
words.longDefinition: Includes vw (viewport width), vh (viewport height), vmin, and vmax. Modern versions also include svh/lvh/dvh to handle mobile browser address bars.
words.techContext: Commonly used for full-screen hero sections or scaling typography based on screen size.
words.exampleSentence: We set the hero section to 100vh to ensure it always fills the entire height of the user's screen.
words.connector: Think of viewport units like percentages of the window size — they automatically adjust when the user resizes their browser, keeping proportions relative to what they can see.

words.shortDefinition: The syntax used to define the intermediate steps in a CSS animation sequence.
words.longDefinition: The @keyframes rule allows authors to control the intermediate steps in a CSS animation sequence by defining styles for keyframes (or waypoints) along the animation sequence.
words.techContext: Enables complex animations that go beyond simple A-to-B transitions.
words.exampleSentence: I defined a set of keyframes to make the notification icon pulse gently every few seconds.

words.shortDefinition: A naming convention for CSS classes to make them more readable and maintainable.
words.longDefinition: Block Element Modifier. A methodology that helps you to create reusable components and code sharing in frontend development by using a strict naming pattern (e.g., block__element--modifier).
words.techContext: Used to avoid specificity wars and clarify the relationship between HTML elements in a large codebase.
words.exampleSentence: Following the BEM methodology helped us avoid style leakage where one component's CSS accidentally affected another.

words.shortDefinition: A way to control animation speed when changing CSS properties.
words.simpleDefinition: A way to animate CSS property changes smoothly over time — instead of instant changes, properties gradually transition from one value to another.
words.longDefinition: Allows property changes in CSS values to occur smoothly over a specified duration, rather than happening instantaneously.
words.techContext: Used for hover effects, showing/hiding menus, or any simple property change that needs to look 'fluid'.
words.exampleSentence: The transition property made the button's background color fade in gently instead of snapping to the new color.
words.connector: Think of transitions like a dimmer switch — instead of flipping a light on/off instantly, it gradually fades between brightness levels.

words.shortDefinition: A way to control the cascade by grouping styles into explicit layers.
words.longDefinition: The @layer CSS rule allows you to explicitly define the order of precedence for your styles, regardless of their specificity. Styles in later layers override styles in earlier layers.
words.techContext: Used to ensure that 'base' styles or third-party libraries don't accidentally override your 'theme' or 'component' styles due to high specificity.
words.exampleSentence: By using cascading layers, we ensured that our utility classes always take precedence over component styles, even without using !important.
